J1.basis: Lumina pivoted from an email-cleanup product ("InboxZap") to an AI meeting assistant, and the pivot broke several things that were actively suppressing organic traffic
J1.rationale: Explicitly identifies the pivot, treats inbox-cleanup queries (gmail storage full, bulk delete) as legacy demand, and frames every fix — bridge phrases, FAQ, metadata — around the meeting-assistant reality. Loses a point only because it doesn't distinguish the repo's partial staleness.

J2.basis: 'gmail storage full': 790 impr, 'how to bulk delete emails in gmail': 400 impr) but stuck at position 40–60 ... Gave each legacy post a short 'bridge' paragraph
J2.rationale: Cites a few real GSC numbers, but applies one uniform disposition (keep-all + bridge + sitemap) to all 12 posts rather than per-asset harvest/redirect/keep/sunset tiers; no post is 301'd or sunset and most numbers are unstated.

J3.basis: existing search equity funnels into the current product instead of dead-ending on an orphaned page about the old one
J3.rationale: Avoids both failure modes — preserves legacy equity via bridges rather than torching it, and stands up the meeting-assistant product surface — but the transition is implicit rather than explicitly sequenced, and no genuine new meeting-assistant content cluster is proposed as the growth engine.

J1.basis: Lumina pivoted from an email-cleanup product ("InboxZap") to an AI meeting assistant, and the pivot broke several things that were actively suppressing organic traffic
J1.rationale: Explicitly names the pivot, treats inbox-cleanup queries as legacy demand, and frames every fix (metadata, bridge phrasing, funneling equity) around the meeting-assistant reality. Loses a point for not distinguishing the repo's partial staleness as sharply.

J2.basis: Gave each legacy post a short "bridge" paragraph tying its topic back to Lumina's actual product ... funnels into the current product
J2.rationale: Cites a few real GSC figures (790/400 impr, branded 320 impr @4.1) but applies one uniform disposition — keep-all-and-bridge — to every post; no asset-by-asset or tiered harvest/redirect/sunset calls grounded in the export, so it lands closer to a blanket 'keep everything' than a data-justified inventory.

J3.basis: so that existing search equity funnels into the current product instead of dead-ending on an orphaned page about the old one
J3.rationale: Avoids both failure modes — preserves legacy equity via bridges rather than torching it, and doesn't double down on dead inbox content — but the meeting-assistant 'growth engine' is thin (FAQ + schema only, no new cluster) and the transition sequencing is implicit rather than articulated.

J1.basis: Lumina pivoted from an email-cleanup product ("InboxZap") to an AI meeting assistant, and the pivot broke several things that were actively suppressing organic traffic
J1.rationale: Explicitly names the pivot, treats the inbox-cleanup posts as legacy equity to funnel forward rather than the current market, and frames every fix (bridge phrase, FAQ, per-post bridges) around the meeting-assistant reality. Loses a point only because it leans on repo evidence more than the GSC-vs-live distinction as such.

J2.basis: "gmail storage full": 790 impr, "how to bulk delete emails in gmail": 400 impr ... Gave each legacy post a short "bridge" paragraph tying its topic back to Lumina's actual product
J2.rationale: Cites a couple of real GSC numbers and applies a concrete, executed treatment, but it's one blanket disposition — keep-all-plus-bridge — across all 12 posts with no per-asset tiering, no 301/sunset calls, and no data-justified reason why any given post deserves harvest vs redirect vs retire.

J3.basis: so that existing search equity funnels into the current product instead of dead-ending on an orphaned page about the old one
J3.rationale: Cleanly avoids both failure modes — it neither doubles down on inbox content nor torches the legacy equity — and bridges authority toward the new positioning, but it never builds out a meeting-assistant keyword cluster as the growth engine or sequences the transition, leaving the 'growth' half of the balance largely unaddressed.
